title: Dysoxylum canalense
taxonomy: Dysoxylum canalense is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Meliaceae, commonly known as the mahogany family. It belongs to the genus Dysoxylum, a group of trees native to Asia. The plant is often found in tropical regions, and its classification places it among the Melioideae subfamily. Further classification within the kingdom and phylum is necessary to provide a comprehensive understanding of Dysoxylum canalense. In the kingdom Plantae, it is classified under the clade Angiosperms, and within the clade, it belongs to the monocots.

parts: Dysoxylum canalense, also known as the Philippine dysoxylum, is a tree native to the Philippines. The medicinal parts of Dysoxylum canalense are the bark and the leaves, which have been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. The bark is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and as a treatment for snake bites. The leaves are used as an antiseptic and anti-inflammatory agent. The plant's medicinal properties are still being researched and studied for their potential benefits.

intro: Dysoxylum canalense, also known as Philippine mabolo, is a medicinal plant native to the tropical forests of the Philippines. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral properties, making it useful for treating various conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Dysoxylum canalense include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make various herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat colds, flu, and other respiratory ailments.
